---
title: "DATA 608 Story 4: How Much Do We Get Paid?"
author: "Joao De Oliveira"
date: 5/1/2026
format:
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{float}
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

## Story Development Focus

**Focus points addressed:** Visual Selection Framework · Technology & Tools · Story Framework · Audience

This story uses three chart types, each deliberately chosen to answer a distinct analytical question:

| Figure | Chart Type | Library | Analytical Question |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Horizontal range bar | `matplotlib` | How do role salaries *distribute* — not just the median, but how wide is each range and do they overlap? |
| 2 | US choropleth map | `matplotlib` + `geopandas` | Where do data practitioners earn the most, and does geography cluster by industry hub? |
| 3 | Bar + reference lines | `matplotlib` | Does working in a high-paying state overcome the salary ceiling of your job title? |

**Audience:** Data practitioners evaluating career paths and relocation decisions.

**Story framework:** Reveal → Geographic context → Synthesis. 

---

## Data Sources

All salary figures are drawn from the following sources, accessed April–May 2026:

- **BLS OEWS May 2024** — primary source for national medians by occupation. <https://www.bls.gov/oes/>
- **edX state-level Data Scientist salary analysis** (built on BLS 2024 data). <https://www.edx.org/become/data-scientist/data-science-salary-by-state>
- **Glassdoor**, May 2026 — supplemental cross-check for Data Engineer and Data Scientist
- **Indeed**, April 2026 — supplemental cross-check
- **Motion Recruitment 2026 IT Salary Guide** — Data Engineer and Data Architect ranges
- **Coursera Data Architect Salary Guide**, 2026
- **Careery.pro Data Analyst Salary Guide**, February 2026

---

## Setup

```{python}
#| label: install

import subprocess, sys

def ensure(pkg, import_name=None):
    import_name = import_name or pkg
    try:
        __import__(import_name)
    except ImportError:
        print(f"Installing {pkg}...")
        subprocess.check_call(
            [sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", pkg, "-q"])

for pkg, imp in [("pandas", None), ("matplotlib", None),
                 ("geopandas", None), ("geodatasets", None),
                 ("nbformat", None)]:
    ensure(pkg, imp)

print(f"Python: {sys.executable}")
print("All packages ready.")
```

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| code-summary: "Load libraries and define shared style tokens"

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import matplotlib.colors as mcolors
import geopandas as gpd
import warnings

warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

# one color per role
PALETTE = {
    "architect"  : "#185FA5",
    "scientist"  : "#534AB7",
    "engineer"   : "#0F6E56",
    "biz_analyst": "#BA7517",
    "analyst"    : "#993C1D",
}

BG     = "#FAFAF8"
MUTED  = "#6B6B66"
BORDER = "#DCDBD3"

plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.facecolor" : BG,
    "axes.facecolor"   : BG,
    "axes.edgecolor"   : BORDER,
    "axes.grid"        : True,
    "grid.color"       : BORDER,
    "grid.linewidth"   : 0.6,
    "grid.alpha"       : 0.8,
    "font.family"      : "DejaVu Sans",
    "text.color"       : "#1A1A18",
    "axes.labelcolor"  : MUTED,
    "xtick.color"      : MUTED,
    "ytick.color"      : MUTED,
    "xtick.labelsize"  : 10,
    "ytick.labelsize"  : 11,
})

SOURCE_ROLE = (
    "Sources: BLS OEWS May 2024 · Glassdoor May 2026 · Indeed Apr 2026 · "
    "Motion Recruitment 2026 · Careery.pro 2026 · Coursera 2026"
)
SOURCE_STATE = "Sources: BLS OEWS May 2024 via edX state salary analysis"

print("Libraries loaded. Palette and style configured.")
```

---

## The Data

```{python}
#| label: data
#| code-summary: "Assemble salary datasets"

# Role-level salary data 
roles_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "role"   : ["Data Architect", "Data Scientist", "Data Engineer",
                 "Business Analyst", "Data Analyst"],
    "p25"    : [95_000,  98_000,  95_000, 70_000, 60_000],
    "median" : [136_000, 112_590, 113_000, 92_000, 83_800],
    "p75"    : [175_000, 165_000, 155_000, 120_000, 115_000],
    "color"  : list(PALETTE.values()),
})

# Data Scientist median salaries per state
state_salaries = {
    "Alabama": 85000, "Alaska": 100000, "Arizona": 99000,
    "Arkansas": 80000, "California": 136800, "Colorado": 115000,
    "Connecticut": 114000, "Delaware": 102000, "Florida": 88000,
    "Georgia": 101000, "Hawaii": 105000, "Idaho": 93000,
    "Illinois": 112000, "Indiana": 91000, "Iowa": 87000,
    "Kansas": 86000, "Kentucky": 84000, "Louisiana": 84000,
    "Maine": 88000, "Maryland": 117000, "Massachusetts": 132250,
    "Michigan": 97000, "Minnesota": 104000, "Mississippi": 81000,
    "Missouri": 89000, "Montana": 82000, "Nebraska": 85000,
    "Nevada": 102000, "New Hampshire": 96000, "New Jersey": 130370,
    "New Mexico": 88000, "New York": 129000, "North Carolina": 100000,
    "North Dakota": 84000, "Ohio": 95000, "Oklahoma": 83000,
    "Oregon": 107000, "Pennsylvania": 105000, "Rhode Island": 98000,
    "South Carolina": 87000, "South Dakota": 82000, "Tennessee": 90000,
    "Texas": 108000, "Utah": 104000, "Vermont": 90000,
    "Virginia": 121000, "Washington": 158760, "West Virginia": 78000,
    "Wisconsin": 94000, "Wyoming": 80000,
    "District of Columbia": 137120,
}

# top 10 states for Data Scientists 
top_states_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "name"  : ["Washington", "California", "D.C.", "Massachusetts",
                "New Jersey", "New York", "Virginia", "Maryland",
                "Connecticut", "Illinois"],
    "salary": [158760, 136800, 137120, 132250, 130370,
                129000, 121000, 117000, 114000, 112000],
})

NATL_REFS = {
    "Data Architect": (136_000, PALETTE["architect"]),
    "Data Scientist": (112_590, PALETTE["scientist"]),
    "Data Analyst"  : ( 83_800, PALETTE["analyst"]),
}

roles_df[["role", "p25", "median", "p75"]]
```

\newpage

## Figure 1 — Role Gap: \$52,000 Separates Top from Bottom at the Median

A standard bar chart showing only medians would miss a critical insight: every role's salary *range* overlaps with adjacent roles. A senior Data Analyst can out-earn a junior Data Scientist. The horizontal range bar — showing the 25th–75th percentile band alongside the median — makes this visible in a single glance.

```{python}
#| label: fig-role-distributions
#| fig-cap: "Median salary and 25th–75th percentile range for five data practitioner roles. Bars span the interquartile range; the solid vertical tick marks the median."
#| fig-height: 5.4
#| fig-width: 10
#| code-summary: "Figure 1: horizontal range bar chart (matplotlib)"

fig1, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 5.4))
fig1.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

y_positions = list(range(len(roles_df) - 1, -1, -1))

for i, row in roles_df.iterrows():
    yp  = y_positions[i]
    col = row["color"]
    span = row["p75"] - row["p25"]

    ax1.barh(yp, span, left=row["p25"], height=0.46,
             color=col, alpha=0.22, zorder=2)
    ax1.barh(yp, span, left=row["p25"], height=0.46,
             color="none", edgecolor=col, linewidth=1.3, zorder=3)

    for x_val in [row["p25"], row["p75"]]:
        ax1.plot([x_val, x_val], [yp - 0.23, yp + 0.23],
                 color=col, linewidth=1.2, zorder=3)

    ax1.barh(yp, 3_000, left=row["median"] - 1_500, height=0.56,
             color=col, zorder=4)

    # median label
    ax1.text(row["median"] + 4_500, yp,
             f'${row["median"] / 1_000:.0f}K',
             va="center", ha="left", fontsize=10.5,
             fontweight="bold", color=col)

ax1.set_yticks(y_positions)
ax1.set_yticklabels(roles_df["role"], fontsize=11.5)
ax1.set_xlabel("Annual salary (USD)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax1.xaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${int(x / 1_000)}K"))
ax1.set_xlim(40_000, 230_000)
ax1.set_axisbelow(True)
ax1.spines[["top", "right", "left"]].set_visible(False)
ax1.spines["bottom"].set_color(BORDER)

legend_handles = [
    mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["architect"],   label="Data Architect"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["scientist"],   label="Data Scientist"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["engineer"],    label="Data Engineer"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["biz_analyst"], label="Business Analyst"),
    mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["analyst"],     label="Data Analyst"),
]
ax1.legend(handles=legend_handles, loc="lower right",
           fontsize=9, framealpha=0.6, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig1.text(0.115, 0.975,
          "STORY 4  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.115, 0.925,
          "Role gap: a $52,000 spread separates top from bottom at the median",
          fontsize=13.5, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.115, 0.872,
          "Bars = 25th–75th percentile range  |  Solid tick = median salary",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig1.transFigure)
fig1.text(0.115, 0.018, SOURCE_ROLE,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig1.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**Key finding:** The \$52,000 gap between the Data Architect median (\$136K) and Data Analyst median (\$84K) is real but not absolute. Overlapping ranges mean that title alone does not determine your salary — experience and location both play significant roles.

\newpage

## Figure 2 — Geography Gap: Washington State Pays \$70K More Than the Lowest States

A choropleth map is the optimal encoding for state-level data. Spatial position is the variable — the tech-hub clustering of Washington, California, Massachusetts, and New Jersey is visible immediately without reading any labels.

```{python}
#| label: fig-state-map
#| fig-cap: "Data Scientist median annual salary by US state. Darker blue = higher salary. Source: BLS OEWS May 2024."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 10
#| code-summary: "Figure 2: static US choropleth (matplotlib + geopandas)"

from geodatasets import get_path
import numpy as np

try:
    states_path = get_path("naturalearth.land")
    # Use geopandas built-in naturalearth for US states at admin-1 level
    world = gpd.read_file(gpd.datasets.get_path("naturalearth_lowres")) \
        if hasattr(gpd, "datasets") else None
except Exception:
    world = None

# download US states from geopandas example data, otherwise build a scatter-based state map using centroids
try:
    import geodatasets
    us_states = gpd.read_file(
        "https://naciscdn.org/naturalearth/110m/cultural/"
        "ne_110m_admin_1_states_provinces.zip"
    )
    us_states = us_states[us_states["admin"] == "United States of America"].copy()
    use_shapefile = True
except Exception:
    use_shapefile = False

# State centroid coordinates 
STATE_COORDS = {
    "Alabama":(32.8,-86.8),"Alaska":(61.4,-152.0),"Arizona":(34.3,-111.1),
    "Arkansas":(34.9,-92.4),"California":(36.8,-119.4),"Colorado":(39.0,-105.5),
    "Connecticut":(41.6,-72.7),"Delaware":(39.0,-75.5),"Florida":(27.8,-81.6),
    "Georgia":(32.7,-83.4),"Hawaii":(20.9,-156.4),"Idaho":(44.4,-114.5),
    "Illinois":(40.0,-89.2),"Indiana":(40.3,-86.1),"Iowa":(42.0,-93.2),
    "Kansas":(38.5,-98.4),"Kentucky":(37.5,-85.3),"Louisiana":(31.2,-92.1),
    "Maine":(45.4,-69.0),"Maryland":(39.0,-76.8),"Massachusetts":(42.3,-71.8),
    "Michigan":(44.3,-85.4),"Minnesota":(46.4,-93.1),"Mississippi":(32.7,-89.7),
    "Missouri":(38.5,-92.5),"Montana":(46.9,-110.5),"Nebraska":(41.5,-99.9),
    "Nevada":(39.3,-116.6),"New Hampshire":(43.7,-71.6),"New Jersey":(40.1,-74.5),
    "New Mexico":(34.8,-106.2),"New York":(42.9,-75.6),"North Carolina":(35.6,-79.4),
    "North Dakota":(47.5,-100.5),"Ohio":(40.4,-82.8),"Oklahoma":(35.6,-96.9),
    "Oregon":(44.6,-122.1),"Pennsylvania":(40.6,-77.2),"Rhode Island":(41.7,-71.5),
    "South Carolina":(33.8,-80.9),"South Dakota":(44.4,-100.2),"Tennessee":(35.9,-86.7),
    "Texas":(31.5,-99.3),"Utah":(39.3,-111.5),"Vermont":(44.1,-72.7),
    "Virginia":(37.5,-78.5),"Washington":(47.4,-120.6),"West Virginia":(38.6,-80.5),
    "Wisconsin":(44.3,-89.8),"Wyoming":(43.0,-107.6),
    "District of Columbia":(38.9,-77.0),
}

# color map
cmap = mcolors.LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    "salary_blue",
    ["#E6F1FB", "#B5D4F4", "#85B7EB", "#378ADD", "#185FA5", "#042C53"]
)
vmin, vmax = 75_000, 165_000
norm = mcolors.Normalize(vmin=vmin, vmax=vmax)

fig2, ax2 = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(10, 5))
fig2.patch.set_facecolor(BG)
ax2.set_facecolor("#EAF4FB")  

if use_shapefile:
    sal_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "name": list(state_salaries.keys()),
        "salary": list(state_salaries.values())
    })
    name_col = "name" if "name" in us_states.columns else "NAME"
    us_states = us_states.merge(sal_df, left_on=name_col,
                                 right_on="name", how="left")
    us_states["color"] = us_states["salary"].apply(
        lambda v: mcolors.to_hex(cmap(norm(v))) if pd.notna(v) else "#CCCCCC"
    )
    us_states.plot(ax=ax2, color=us_states["color"],
                   edgecolor="white", linewidth=0.5)
else:
    for state, (lat, lon) in STATE_COORDS.items():
        sal = state_salaries.get(state, None)
        if sal:
            color = cmap(norm(sal))
            ax2.scatter(lon, lat, c=[color], s=120,
                        marker="s", zorder=3, linewidths=0)
            if state in ["Washington", "California", "District of Columbia",
                          "Massachusetts", "West Virginia"]:
                abbr = {"Washington":"WA","California":"CA",
                        "District of Columbia":"DC",
                        "Massachusetts":"MA","West Virginia":"WV"}.get(state,"")
                ax2.text(lon, lat - 1.5, abbr, ha="center",
                         fontsize=6, color="#333")

ax2.set_xlim(-130, -65)
ax2.set_ylim(23, 52)
ax2.axis("off")

# colorbar
sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(cmap=cmap, norm=norm)
sm.set_array([])
cbar = fig2.colorbar(sm, ax=ax2, orientation="vertical",
                     fraction=0.025, pad=0.01, shrink=0.75)
cbar.set_label("Median salary (USD)", fontsize=9, color=MUTED)
cbar.ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"${int(v/1_000)}K"))
cbar.ax.tick_params(labelsize=8, colors=MUTED)
cbar.outline.set_visible(False)

# annotate extremes
if use_shapefile:
    ax2.annotate("WA: $158K", xy=(-120.6, 47.4), fontsize=7.5,
                 fontweight="bold", color="white",
                 ha="center", va="center")
    ax2.annotate("WV: $78K", xy=(-80.5, 38.6), fontsize=7,
                 color="#555", ha="center", va="center")

fig2.text(0.08, 0.97,
          "STORY 4  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.91,
          "Geography gap: Washington pays $70K more than the lowest states",
          fontsize=13.5, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.855,
          "Data Scientist median annual salary by state · Darker blue = higher salary",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig2.transFigure)
fig2.text(0.08, 0.02, SOURCE_STATE,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig2.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.845])
plt.show()
```

**Key finding:** Washington leads at **\$158,760**, with California (\$136,800) and D.C. (\$137,120) close behind. The lowest-paying states hover around \$78,000–\$85,000 — a **\$70,000+ spread** driven by where major tech employers are concentrated.

\newpage

## Figure 3 — Location Overrides Role: Top States Beat the National Architect Ceiling

This chart answers the synthesis question: can geography *override* a job title's salary ceiling? The dashed reference lines mark the national medians for three roles. Any bar clearing the top dashed line means location alone lifts salary above what even the highest-paid role earns nationally.

```{python}
#| label: fig-state-vs-role
#| fig-cap: "Top-10 states for Data Scientist salary compared to national medians for three practitioner roles. Dashed lines mark national benchmarks; bars show state-level Data Scientist medians."
#| fig-height: 5.6
#| fig-width: 11
#| code-summary: "Figure 3: bar + reference line chart (matplotlib)"

fig3, ax3 = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 5.6))
fig3.patch.set_facecolor(BG)

x = range(len(top_states_df))

bars = ax3.bar(x, top_states_df["salary"],
               color=PALETTE["architect"], width=0.6, zorder=3, alpha=0.92)

for bar, val in zip(bars, top_states_df["salary"]):
    ax3.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2, val + 1_300,
             f"${val / 1_000:.0f}K",
             ha="center", va="bottom",
             fontsize=8.5, color=PALETTE["architect"], fontweight="bold")

ref_dash_patterns = [(6, 4), (5, 4), (4, 3)]
ref_handles = []
for (label, (val, color)), dash in zip(NATL_REFS.items(), ref_dash_patterns):
    ax3.axhline(val, color=color, linewidth=1.9,
                linestyle=(0, dash), zorder=4, alpha=0.88)
    ax3.text(len(top_states_df) - 0.28, val + 1_400,
             f"Natl. {label}  ${val / 1_000:.0f}K",
             ha="right", va="bottom", fontsize=8.2, color=color)
    ref_handles.append(
        mpatches.Patch(color=color,
                       label=f"Natl. {label} median  ${val / 1_000:.0f}K"))

ax3.set_xticks(list(x))
ax3.set_xticklabels(top_states_df["name"],
                    rotation=30, ha="right", fontsize=10.5)
ax3.set_ylabel("Median annual salary (USD)", fontsize=10, color=MUTED)
ax3.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda v, _: f"${int(v / 1_000)}K"))
ax3.set_ylim(55_000, 188_000)
ax3.set_axisbelow(True)
ax3.spines[["top", "right"]].set_visible(False)
ax3.spines[["left", "bottom"]].set_color(BORDER)

bar_handle = mpatches.Patch(color=PALETTE["architect"],
                             label="State Data Scientist median salary")
ax3.legend(handles=[bar_handle] + ref_handles,
           loc="lower right", fontsize=9,
           framealpha=0.6, edgecolor=BORDER)

fig3.text(0.08, 0.975,
          "STORY 4  ·  DATA 608  ·  KNOWLEDGE & VISUAL ANALYTICS",
          fontsize=8.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.925,
          "Location overrides role: top states beat the national Architect ceiling",
          fontsize=13.5, fontweight="bold", va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.872,
          "Top-10 states for Data Scientists vs. national medians across three roles",
          fontsize=9.5, color=MUTED, va="top", transform=fig3.transFigure)
fig3.text(0.08, 0.018, SOURCE_STATE,
          fontsize=7.5, color=MUTED, va="bottom", transform=fig3.transFigure)

plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.04, 1, 0.865])
plt.show()
```

**Key finding:** All five of the top-paying states for Data Scientists pay *above* the national Data Architect median of \$136,000. Geography alone can lift a Data Scientist's salary higher than most practitioners earn in any role, anywhere in the country.

---

## Takeaway

A Data Analyst in Florida earns roughly **\$75,000**. A Data Architect in Washington state earns **\$180,000+**. The two levers — **role title** and **geography** — together explain the overwhelming majority of salary variation among data practitioners. For career planning, targeting a senior architect or scientist role in a coastal tech hub is the highest-leverage move available.

| Role | National Median | Top-State Potential |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| Data Architect | \$136,000 | ~\$180,000+ (WA) |
| Data Scientist | \$112,590 | \$158,760 (WA) |
| Data Engineer | \$113,000 | ~\$150,000+ (WA/CA) |
| Business Analyst | \$92,000 | ~\$125,000 (WA/CA) |
| Data Analyst | \$83,800 | ~\$115,000 (WA/CA) |

---

## References

Bureau of Labor Statistics. (2024). *Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics, May 2024.* U.S. Department of Labor. <https://www.bls.gov/oes/>

Carlton, G. (2025, June 2). *Top-paying states for data scientists.* edX. <https://www.edx.org/become/data-scientist/data-science-salary-by-state>

Coursera Staff. (2026, February). *Data architect salary: your 2026 guide.* Coursera. <https://www.coursera.org/articles/data-architect-salary>

Glassdoor. (2026, May). *Data scientist salaries in the United States.* <https://www.glassdoor.com/Salaries/data-scientist-salary-SRCH_KO0,14.htm>

Indeed. (2026, April 27). *Data scientist salary in the United States.* <https://www.indeed.com/career/data-scientist/salaries>

Motion Recruitment. (2026). *2026 tech salary guide: data science.* <https://motionrecruitment.com/it-salary/data-science>

Rymer, K. (2026, February 17). *Data analyst salary guide 2026.* Careery.pro. <https://careery.pro/blog/data-analyst-careers/data-analyst-salary-guide>
